reason: The passage presents God as the primary decision-maker, determining to liberate Israel and strike Egypt. Moses, as the leader, is commissioned to execute these decisions. The narrative structure shows God commanding Moses to gather the elders and approach Pharaoh, with the elders and other Israelites portrayed as recipients or followers of these decisions rather than independent decision-makers. Even Moses' decision to turn aside to view the burning bush (וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אָסֻֽרָה־ נָּא וְאֶרְאֶה) is presented as divinely orchestrated (וַיּרְא יְהוָה כִּי סָר לִרְאוֹת). The Egyptian women's provision of items and the Pharaoh's eventual release of the people are portrayed as consequences of God's plan and the leaders' actions rather than as independent decisions by ordinary people.
